name: Donnelly
short_description: BLM public land near Donnelly offers free rockhounding for quartz and jasper specimens in a scenic mountain valley setting at 4857 ft elevation.
description:
Donnelly sits in the heart of Valley County's diverse geology, where the Cascade Range foothills create excellent conditions for finding quality quartz, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. This BLM land allows recreational collectors to search at no cost with straightforward personal-use rules. The terrain is relatively flat to gently rolling, making it accessible for most rockhounders while still offering plenty of undisturbed ground to explore. The area benefits from volcanic and hydrothermal activity that has created rich mineral deposits across the landscape. Spring and fall are ideal visiting seasons when temperatures are moderate and visibility is excellent.

The Donnelly area developed as a small mountain community centered around timber and mining interests, and the surrounding BLM lands remain prime territory for finding colorful mineral specimens. Local washes and exposed hillsides frequently yield agate nodules and jasper fragments that have weathered out of host rock. The relatively flat topography makes this an excellent introductory rockhounding location for families and beginners who want to collect without technical equipment or extreme physical demands.

Water access and services are available in nearby Donnelly town, and the proximity to central Idaho's outdoor recreation corridor makes this a practical stop for rockhounders touring the region. The elevation means winter access can be limited by snow, but the site remains open year-round when conditions permit.
history: This region lies within the Cascade Range volcanic province where Tertiary-age volcanism and subsequent hydrothermal circulation deposited silica-rich fluids that crystallized into quartz, chalcedony, and jasper throughout the area. Local mining activity in the late 1800s and early 1900s focused primarily on gold and silver, but rockhounders today benefit from the exposed mineralized zones that mining operations and natural erosion have revealed.
tips: Search along dry washes and eroded hillsides where specimens naturally concentrate through weathering and water transport. Bring a small hand hammer and chisel to extract specimens from friable matrix, and use a 5-gallon bucket to haul finds back to your vehicle. Look for translucent or banded quartz and jasper fragments ranging from thumbnail to fist size, and check exposed banks where color contrasts stand out against weathered rock.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4857 feet in a mountain valley setting with relatively flat to gently rolling terrain. Scattered ponderosa pine and sagebrush vegetation with open ground suitable for walking. Minimal elevation change across most collecting areas, making footing stable and hiking straightforward. Most productive areas are within easy walking distance of vehicle parking.
best_season: May - September. Spring and early summer provide optimal conditions with moderate temperatures and good visibility. Late September through early October offers pleasant fall weather. Winter snowfall can close access, and summer afternoons can be warm, making morning and evening collecting preferred.
written_directions: From Donnelly, head north on Valley County Road 48 for approximately 3 miles. Look for BLM boundary markers and unsigned pullouts on either side of the road near exposed hillsides and wash areas. Park in designated pullout areas and hike to exposed rocky terrain. GPS coordinates 44.74446, -116.058351 mark a central access point with good foot access to mineral-bearing ground.
road_conditions: Valley County Road 48 is graded dirt and gravel, passable for standard vehicles in dry conditions. May become rutted or muddy after heavy rain or snowmelt.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along valley roads serve as parking areas. No developed parking lot, but multiple turnouts accommodate 2-3 vehicles each. Roadside parking typical for BLM access.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only loose specimens and material you can hand-extract. Leave no trace and respect BLM land designations.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation equipment
Hand tools only (hammer, chisel, pry bar)
No collection from enclosed or restricted areas
Repack any holes and leave area clean
nearest_services: Donnelly, approximately 3 miles south, has general store, gas, and basic services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved road to parking area passable for standard vehicles. Walking distances short to moderate, making this suitable for visitors with limited mobility who can manage uneven ground. No wheelchair accessibility on BLM terrain.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Donnelly, Idaho?
faq.answer: The Donnelly area produces quartz, jasper, agate, and chalcedony specimens. Quartz is most abundant and appears in both clear and banded varieties. Jasper typically shows red, brown, or banded coloring, and agate nodules are prized for their translucent banding. All four gem types weather out of hillsides and concentrate in dry washes where collectors can find them easily.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Donnelly BLM land?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding is completely free on Donnelly BLM land. There are no permits required and no fees to collect. You simply need to follow personal-use rules: limit yourself to 25 lbs per day, use hand tools only, and avoid commercial collection.

faq.question: What is the best time to go rockhounding in Donnelly, Idaho?
faq.answer: May through September is ideal, with May-June and September offering the most pleasant temperatures. Winter snowfall can restrict access, and summer afternoons can get warm. Early morning and evening collecting during summer months is most comfortable. Spring and fall provide excellent visibility and moderate weather.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks at Donnelly?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on Donnelly BLM land. The site operates under standard BLM personal-use collection rules, which allow you to collect without advance permission as long as you stay within the 25 lb daily limit and use hand tools only.

faq.question: Is Donnelly rockhounding suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Donnelly is an excellent beginner and family rockhounding location. The terrain is relatively flat with easy walking, specimens are accessible by hand collection, and no special skills or equipment are required. Children age 6 and up can participate comfortably. This site is perfect for introducing family members to rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools do I need for rockhounding at Donnelly, Idaho?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand hammer and chisel to extract specimens from friable matrix rock. A 5-gallon bucket helps carry your finds, and a backpack keeps your hands free for hiking. Wear sturdy hiking boots for uneven ground, bring sunscreen and water, and consider a magnifying glass to identify specimens. No motorized equipment is allowed.

meta_title: Donnelly Rockhounding - Idaho BLM Free Collecting
meta_description: Find quartz, jasper, and agate at Donnelly, Idaho on free BLM land. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Easy family rockhounding.
